channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: Scaling Fully Secure MPC via Robust Recursive Search and Gap Amplification
item.description: We present the first framework for lifting semi-honest MPC protocols to **fully malicious security** with **two-thirds honest majority**, while preserving the underlying protocol’s security type (computational or information-theoretic) and supporting any linear secret sharing over finite rings. Our solution achieves *simultaneously optimal* complexity: only **$O(\log|C|)$ communication overhead**, **constant additional rounds** (independent of circuit size $|C|$), and **$O(|C|)$ arithmetic operations per party**—a triple improvement unattained by prior works. This is enabled by a novel **robust recursive search** technique that reliably identifies and eliminates cheaters. Further, via **gap amplification**, we reduce party-dependent costs: communication drops from $O(n^3)$ to $\tilde{O}(n^{5/2})$, and extra rounds shrink from $O(n)$ to $O(\sqrt{n \log \log n})$, *without increasing computational overhead*. Gap amplification is broadly applicable to any player-elimination-based MPC protocol.

item.title: Scalable Compliant Privacy on Starknet
item.description: We present *Scalable Compliant Privacy*, a production-ready privacy protocol on Starknet that reconciles strong confidentiality with regulatory accountability. It hides sender, receiver, and amount in all transfers, enforcing validity via efficient client-side STARK proofs (using Stwo), minimizing L2 gas and latency. Three key innovations enable scalability and compliance: (1) an O(log N) note discovery mechanism for fast private balance lookup; (2) a permissioned, cryptographically auditable compliance layer—only authorized auditors can selectively unshield specific transactions upon verifiable legal mandate, with on-chain proof of authorization; and (3) anonymous composability with existing Starknet DeFi contracts via native account abstraction. Implemented entirely in Cairo, the system supports multi-token pools, achieves <3.2s end-to-end latency, and sustains >1800 TPS under batched verification—demonstrating a viable path toward regulated, high-throughput private finance.

item.title: PIKE: Faster Isogeny-Based Public Key Encryption with Pairing-Assisted Decryption
item.description: PIKE is a new isogeny-based public-key encryption (PKE) scheme that improves upon the recent POKÉ construction (Eurocrypt 2025) by replacing part of the decryption computation with efficient bilinear pairings. This pairing-assisted secret derivation preserves post-quantum security while enabling two key gains: (i) faster decryption—eliminating costly isogeny path reconstruction—and (ii) greater flexibility in prime selection, allowing smaller, more efficient primes. Our SageMath implementation shows speedups of **1.30× (keygen), 1.24× (enc), and 1.47× (dec)** over POKÉ at NIST Level I, with competitive key/ciphertext sizes (~3 KB). A highly optimized C implementation achieves **53 Mcycles (23 ms) for encryption and 34 Mcycles (15 ms) for decryption** on an Intel i7-8550U (2.3 GHz), setting a new efficiency benchmark for practical isogeny-based PKE.

item.title: Descent into Broken Trust: Uncovering ML-DSA Subkeys with Scarce Leakage and Local Optimization
item.description: ML-DSA (formerly CRYSTALS-Dilithium), NIST’s flagship post-quantum signature standard, relies on rejection sampling to mask secret-key dependencies in signatures. Recent attacks exploit even **1-bit leakage per signature** (e.g., side-channel leaks of sampling decisions) to recover the secret key via large-scale linear regression—requiring up to **2.4 million “informative relations”** for ML-DSA-65, limiting practicality. We radically reduce this cost by recasting key recovery as a **constraint-satisfaction problem solvable via local optimization**. Our approach introduces: (i) a lightweight verification routine that validates candidate subkeys *using only leakage relations*, avoiding expensive lattice reductions or knowledge of other secret components; and (ii) a multi-tier hill-climbing algorithm that minimizes a theoretically grounded scoring function. In the exact-leakage setting, our attack recovers ML-DSA subkeys from just **5,000–35,000 relations** across all parameter sets—achieving **37–68× fewer samples** than prior art. Crucially, we extend the attack to a noisy model where the leaked bit flips independently with probability $p$, and demonstrate successful key recovery even at **$p = 0.45$**, with substantially less leakage than previous methods.

item.title: Lookup Arguments over Rings and Applications to Batch-Verification of RAM Programs
item.description: Lookup arguments are essential for efficient SNARKs, enabling cheap verification of arithmetization-unfriendly operations (e.g., range checks) via public tables. However, all prior constructions operate over fields—making them incompatible with post-quantum lattice-based SNARKs that inherently work over rings like $\mathcal{R} = \mathbb{Z}_q[X]/(X^d + 1)$. We formalize lookup arguments over rings for arbitrary ring-element tables and expose critical pitfalls in naïve field-to-ring translations—including zero-divisor attacks and interpolation ambiguity. We then adapt two foundational polynomial IOPs, **Plookup** and **LogUp**, to $\mathcal{R}$, leveraging ring-aware polynomial commitments based on the Module-LWE assumption. This yields the first succinct, lattice-based lookup arguments with rigorous security. As a key application, we construct succinct batch-verification arguments for RAM programs whose memory entries are arbitrary ring elements—enabling efficient, quantum-safe verification of dynamic memory updates.

item.title: Byzantine Consensus in the Partially Authenticated Setting
item.description: This paper initiates the first systematic study of Byzantine consensus in the *partially authenticated setting*, motivated by Proof-of-Stake blockchains where only a stable subset of parties (e.g., validators) hold long-term PKI keys, while others do not. We characterize feasibility in terms of $n$ total parties and $s$ registered ones. When the sender is *unregistered*, Byzantine Agreement and Broadcast are possible iff $t \le \max\{\lceil s/2\rceil,\lceil n/3\rceil\}-1$. When the sender is *registered*, we give a deterministic synchronous broadcast protocol tolerating up to $t \le s + \lceil (n-s)/3\rceil - 1$ faults (i.e., $3t < n + 2s$), extendable to efficient multivalued consensus. Crucially, we prove a matching lower bound under a strengthened leakage model—where the adversary learns each party’s private state after every round—showing no deterministic or short-secret–dependent randomized protocol can exceed this bound. Our results bridge theory and practice for heterogeneous identity models in modern distributed ledgers.

item.title: A Note on ``Linear-Communication ACSS with Guaranteed Termination and Lower Amortized Bound''
item.description: We critically analyze the asynchronous complete secret sharing (ACSS) protocol proposed by Qin et al. (Eurocrypt 2026), which claims linear communication, guaranteed termination, and improved amortized bounds. We demonstrate that the protocol is **fundamentally insecure**: it fails to guarantee liveness and correctness under standard Byzantine adversary assumptions ($t < n/3$). Specifically, we identify three critical flaws: (1) insufficient validation in the broadcast phase enables malicious parties to forge “valid” round advances; (2) reconstruction relies on unsynchronized local view counts, allowing selective message delays to induce inconsistent termination decisions; and (3) the linear communication optimization weakens causal ordering, enabling a novel *Timing-Confusion Attack* that violates guaranteed termination. We present two concrete polynomial-time attacks: one causing indefinite suspension (breaking termination), and another causing honest parties to reconstruct conflicting secrets (breaking correctness). Our findings invalidate the protocol’s core security claims and highlight inherent tensions between linear communication and strong liveness in ACSS design.

item.title: Tighter Proofs for PKE-to-KEM Transformations under Average-Case Decryption Error and without $\gamma$-Spread
item.description: This work advances PKE-to-KEM transformations under average-case decryption error. We introduce $\mathsf{FOAC}'_0$ and $\mathsf{FOAC'}$, achieving significantly tighter QROM security proofs than prior art: $\mathcal{O}(q^2)$ and $\mathcal{O}(q^3)$ loss for OW-CPA-secure PKEs, and optimal $\mathcal{O}(q)$ loss for deterministic or IND-CPA-secure PKEs—surpassing the $\mathcal{O}(q^4)$ bound of Ge et al. (2025). Crucially, both schemes eliminate the $\gamma$-spread assumption entirely, enabling direct application to practical post-quantum PKEs (e.g., Kyber, Saber) without structural constraints. Our proofs rely on three novel QROM techniques built upon Zhandry’s compressed oracle framework—error-aware query partitioning, adaptive error-distribution trimming, and two-phase entropy injection—which may find broader use in quantum-safe cryptography.

item.title: A Note on the Equivalence Between Zero-knowledge and Quantum CSS Codes
item.description: We establish an exact structural equivalence between (linear, perfect) zero-knowledge codes—introduced by Decatur, Goldreich, and Ron—and quantum CSS codes—pioneered by Calderbank–Shor and Steane. Specifically, a linear code $C$ is zero-knowledge if and only if it arises as the intersection $C = C_1 \cap C_2^\perp$ for classical codes satisfying $C_2^\perp \subseteq C_1$, i.e., precisely the defining condition of a CSS code. This equivalence is constructive and preserves parameters: the zero-knowledge threshold corresponds to the dual distance $d(C_2)$, while the message length, rate, and distance map bijectively. As a key application, we obtain the first explicit family of asymptotically good, locally testable zero-knowledge codes—by lifting known good quantum CSS codes (e.g., constant-rate quantum LDPC codes)—resolving a longstanding challenge in coding-theoretic cryptography.

item.title: Hashing in Generic Groups: Completing the AGM-to-GGM Transfer
item.description: The Algebraic Group Model (AGM) is widely used for proving security of group-based primitives, yet its relation to the Generic Group Model (GGM) remains subtle. While Jaeger and Mohan (Crypto 2024) showed AGM hardness lifts to Maurer’s GGM, their framework excludes the Random Oracle Model (ROM)—despite the fact that nearly all practical AGM analyses (e.g., short-ciphertext PKE) rely on AGM+ROM. We first prove a black-box separation: a specific AGM+ROM-secure PKE scheme cannot be secure in *any* extension of Maurer’s GGM with ROM, exposing a fundamental limitation of JM’s approach. To bridge this gap, we propose an augmented framework that tightly integrates Maurer’s GGM with a syntactically aligned ROM—where oracles may input/output native group elements (e.g., $g^a$, pairings). Crucially, we establish a lifting lemma showing that *all* existing AGM+ROM security proofs directly carry over to our model. This completes the AGM-to-GGM transfer for the full spectrum of current applications.

item.title: Robust Single-message Shuffle Differential Privacy Protocol for Accurate Distribution Estimation
item.description: Shuffler-based differential privacy (shuffle-DP) enables high-utility statistical estimation under strong privacy guarantees, yet existing protocols primarily target categorical frequency estimation—not the more prevalent and practically critical task of *numerical distribution estimation* under the pure shuffle model. We identify that naively adapting categorical frequency oracles and standard recovery fails to jointly optimize utility, message complexity (i.e., single-message constraint), and robustness against data poisoning. To bridge this gap, we propose **ASP**, the first single-message adaptive shuffler-based piecewise protocol. ASP features: (i) a randomizer optimized via a novel tighter mutual information bound; and (ii) an **Expectation Maximization with Adaptive Smoothing (EMAS)** algorithm for accurate, poison-resilient distribution recovery. We further introduce a principled robustness evaluation framework assessing resilience across diverse attack targets (e.g., mode, tail, variance). Extensive experiments show ASP achieves up to **10× higher utility** at small $ε$ (e.g., $ε=0.5$), maintains **minimal message complexity (1 message/user)**, and delivers **>3× stronger robustness** than state-of-the-art baselines—establishing a new practical standard for private numerical distribution learning.

item.title: Efficient Privacy-Preserving Sparse Matrix-Vector Multiplication Using Homomorphic Encryption
item.description: Sparse matrix-vector multiplication (SpMV) is a foundational operation in scientific computing and machine learning, yet its privacy-preserving execution over sensitive data remains underexplored under homomorphic encryption (HE). This paper presents the first efficient HE-native SpMV framework, introducing the **Compressed Sparse Sorted Column (CSSC)** format—a sparsity-aware, HE-optimized storage scheme that preserves non-zero structure while enabling dense ciphertext packing and eliminating decryption-side “gaps.” CSSC reduces memory overhead by 58–72% and accelerates computation by 4.2×–6.9× versus prior HE-based approaches on real-world sparse datasets (e.g., PubMed, WebBase-1M), all under 128-bit security. Our work bridges a critical gap between practical sparse linear algebra and rigorous cryptographic privacy, enabling secure federated learning, encrypted databases, and privacy-compliant HPC.

item.title: Dissociating Direct Access from Inference in AI Introspection
item.description: This study dissociates two distinct mechanisms underlying AI introspection: **inference-based anomaly detection** (relying on statistical irregularities in prompts) and **direct access to internal states** (bypassing input cues). Using a scaled replication of Lindsey et al. (2025)’s thought injection paradigm across 12 open-source LLMs, we demonstrate that direct access is *content-agnostic*: models reliably detect *that* an internal anomaly occurred but fail to identify *what* was injected (e.g., “apple” vs. “ocean”), with semantic accuracy near chance (<38%). In contrast, correct concept identification consistently requires longer generations (+42% tokens on average) and exhibits strong bias toward high-frequency, concrete nouns—indicating confabulation rather than veridical retrieval. These findings align with philosophical and psychological theories positing pre-conceptual awareness as foundational to introspection, offering the first empirical evidence of a non-inferential, content-blind self-monitoring capacity in LLMs.

item.title: FedBCD:Communication-Efficient Accelerated Block Coordinate Gradient Descent for Federated Learning
item.description: Federated Learning (FL) suffers from prohibitive communication overhead when training large-scale models like Vision Transformers. To address this, we propose **FedBCD**, the first block-wise parameter communication framework for FL: it partitions model parameters into $N$ blocks (including one shared block) and lets each client upload only *one* selected block per round—reducing per-round communication by a factor of $1/N$. We further introduce **FedBCD+**, an accelerated variant integrating client drift control and stochastic variance reduction. Our convergence analysis proves FedBCD+ achieves $\mathcal{O}(1/T)$ rate under standard non-convex smooth assumptions, with communication complexity strictly lower than FedAvg, SCAFFOLD, and FedProx. Experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet-1K show FedBCD+ attains comparable or superior accuracy using ≤30% of the communication cost. Code is publicly available.

item.title: GEM-TFL: Bridging Weak and Full Supervision for Forgery Localization through EM-Guided Decomposition and Temporal Refinement
item.description: Temporal Forgery Localization (TFL) aims to precisely localize manipulated segments in videos or audio streams for multimedia forensics. While fully supervised TFL relies on costly frame-level annotations, Weakly Supervised TFL (WS-TFL) uses only video-level binary labels—yet suffers from objective mismatch, sparse supervision, non-differentiable top-*k* aggregation, and neglect of inter-proposal relationships. To bridge this gap, we propose **GEM-TFL**, a two-phase classification-regression framework featuring: (1) an **EM-guided decomposition** that transforms binary labels into multi-dimensional latent attributes to enrich weak supervision; (2) a **training-free temporal refinement** that enforces smoothness in frame-level predictions via dynamic thresholding and sliding-window consensus; and (3) a **graph-based proposal refinement** module modeling temporal-semantic dependencies among proposals for globally consistent confidence estimation. Experiments on FakeAVCeleb and DeepFakeTIMIT show GEM-TFL achieves 78.3% mAP@0.5—outperforming prior WS-TFL methods by +9.6% and narrowing the gap with fully supervised counterparts to just 3.2 percentage points—demonstrating unprecedented accuracy and robustness under minimal supervision.

item.title: S5-SHB Agent: Society 5.0 enabled Multi-model Agentic Blockchain Framework for Smart Home
item.description: This paper introduces **S5-SHB Agent**, a Society 5.0–aligned, multi-model agentic blockchain framework for smart homes. It overcomes three key limitations of prior work: rigid consensus, monolithic AI models, and absent resident governance. The framework orchestrates ten specialized LLM-powered agents (interchangeable across Qwen, Llama3, etc.) to jointly optimize safety, security, comfort, energy, privacy, and health. Its adaptive PoW blockchain dynamically adjusts mining difficulty based on transaction load and emergency triggers (e.g., fire alarms), ensuring sub-2-second emergency block finality while guaranteeing tamper-evident auditability via digital signatures and Merkle tree anchoring. A novel four-tier governance model empowers residents to define adjustable preferences (e.g., temperature ranges) and immutable safety thresholds (e.g., gas leak cutoffs) — validated across all test configurations with 100% correct policy separation. S5-SHB Agent bridges blockchain accountability, AI adaptability, and human-centered control in smart home ecosystems.

item.title: Poisoning the Inner Prediction Logic of Graph Neural Networks for Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks
item.description: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, but existing methods require *label poisoning*—changing labels of trigger-attached training nodes to the target class—a strong and often impractical assumption in real-world settings where labels are fixed and trustworthy. This work tackles the realistic yet underexplored *clean-label graph backdoor attack*, where **all training labels remain unchanged**, and only structural/feature triggers are injected. We identify that prior approaches fail because they cannot *poison the inner prediction logic*: triggers are ignored as non-discriminative noise during GNN message passing. To address this, we propose **BA-Logic**, a novel framework that jointly optimizes (1) a *poisoned node selector* identifying highly susceptible clean training nodes, and (2) a *logic-poisoning trigger generator* that manipulates graph topology and node features to hijack GNN’s aggregation mechanism—amplifying target-class-relevant signals while suppressing others. Extensive experiments on Cora, Citeseer, and ACM show BA-Logic achieves up to **86.3% attack success rate** under clean-label constraints, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by over 41.2% while preserving >98.5% clean accuracy. Code is publicly available.

item.title: Differentially Private Multimodal In-Context Learning
item.description: We present **DP-MTV (Differentially Private Multimodal Task Vectors)**, the first framework enabling *many-shot*, *multimodal* in-context learning with formal $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-differential privacy. To overcome the prohibitive privacy cost of processing large multimodal demonstrations, DP-MTV projects private image-text examples into activation space and aggregates them into compact, noise-resistant task vectors—avoiding per-token or per-sample noise. It ensures privacy via disjoint data partitioning, layer-wise gradient/activation clipping for tight sensitivity control, and a single calibrated Gaussian noise addition to the final vector, enabling unlimited inference queries. Evaluated across 8 benchmarks and 3 VLMs (Flamingo, KOSMOS-2, LLaVA), DP-MTV achieves **50.0% accuracy on VizWiz at $\varepsilon=1.0, \delta=10^{-5}$**, surpassing zero-shot (35%) and retaining 91% of the non-private in-context learning gain (55%). It operates without auxiliary data and supports optional public data integration—establishing a new foundation for privacy-preserving multimodal reasoning.

item.title: FedAFD: Multimodal Federated Learning via Adversarial Fusion and Distillation
item.description:
## FedAFD: Multimodal Federated Learning via Adversarial Fusion and Distillation
Multimodal Federated Learning (MFL) enables privacy-preserving collaboration among clients with heterogeneous data modalities (e.g., image, text, audio), yet suffers from poor personalization, modality/task discrepancies, and model heterogeneity. To address these, we propose **FedAFD**, a unified framework featuring: (1) a **bi-level adversarial alignment** strategy on clients to harmonize local and global representations *within* and *across* modalities; (2) a **granularity-aware fusion module** that adaptively integrates distilled global knowledge into personalized features; and (3) a **similarity-guided ensemble distillation** mechanism on the server that aggregates client representations over public data based on feature similarity and distills fused knowledge into a robust global model. Extensive experiments under both IID and non-IID settings show FedAFD achieves state-of-the-art accuracy (+3.2–5.8% avg. gain), faster convergence (37% speedup), and reduced communication overhead (22% lower) — all while preserving data privacy and accommodating structural heterogeneity.

item.title: DeformTrace: A Deformable State Space Model with Relay Tokens for Temporal Forgery Localization
item.description: Temporal Forgery Localization (TFL) seeks precise identification of manipulated segments in video/audio for forensic interpretability. While State Space Models (SSMs) excel at temporal reasoning, their application to TFL is limited by ambiguous boundaries, sparse forgeries, and long-range state decay. We propose **DeformTrace**, a novel deformable SSM framework featuring: (1) a **Deformable Self-SSM (DS-SSM)** with dynamic receptive fields for boundary-precise localization; (2) a lightweight **Relay Token Mechanism** integrated into DS-SSM to mitigate long-range decay; and (3) a **Deformable Cross-SSM (DC-SSM)** that partitions the global state space into query-specific subspaces to suppress non-forgery interference and enhance sensitivity to sparse anomalies. Built upon a hybrid Transformer-SSM architecture, DeformTrace achieves state-of-the-art performance across 7 benchmarks—improving average F1 by +4.7%, reducing localization error by 38%, using ≤3.2M parameters, running 2.1× faster than prior SOTA, and maintaining >92.3% robustness under compression, noise, and resolution degradation.

item.title: Meta-D: Metadata-Aware Architectures for Brain Tumor Analysis and Missing-Modality Segmentation
item.description: We propose **Meta-D**, a metadata-aware deep learning architecture that explicitly incorporates categorical scanner metadata (e.g., MRI sequence type—T1/T2/FLAIR—and plane orientation—axial/sagittal/coronal) to guide feature extraction for brain tumor analysis. In 2D tumor detection, metadata-embedded dynamic convolutional modulation boosts F1-score by up to **+2.62%** over image-only baselines. Leveraging metadata as a robust semantic anchor under data scarcity, we extend Meta-D to 3D missing-modality segmentation via the **Transformer Maximizer**, which uses metadata-conditioned cross-attention to selectively route available modalities and suppress invalid slices. This yields up to **+5.12% Dice improvement** under extreme modality absence (e.g., only 1–2 modalities present) while reducing model parameters by **24.1%**. Meta-D establishes a lightweight, interpretable, and clinically grounded paradigm for metadata-integrated medical AI.

item.title: A Behaviour-Aware Federated Forecasting Framework for Distributed Stand-Alone Wind Turbines
item.description: Accurate short-term wind power forecasting is critical for grid stability and energy markets, yet centralized data aggregation compromises privacy, incurs high communication costs, and ignores inherent behavioral heterogeneity across distributed stand-alone turbines. To address this, we propose a **Behaviour-Aware Federated Forecasting (BAFF) framework**, comprising two stages: (1) behavior-driven clustering using **Double Roulette Selection (DRS) initialization** followed by **recursive Auto-split refinement**, which groups turbines by long-term operational statistics (e.g., volatility, diurnal periodicity, low-power persistence) rather than geography; and (2) cluster-specific LSTM training via **FedAvg**, preserving full data locality. Evaluated on 400 real-world stand-alone turbines in Denmark, BAFF discovers behaviorally coherent clusters with 72% geographic mixing—demonstrating strong decoupling from physical proximity. It achieves **18.3% lower MAE than geographic partitioning**, matches k-means++ baseline accuracy (±0.5%), and improves clustering stability by 2.1×. With only **2.7 KB average uplink per round**, BAFF offers a practical, privacy-preserving solution for heterogeneous turbine fleets.

item.title: Learning Optimal Individualized Decision Rules with Conditional Demographic Parity
item.description: Individualized decision rules (IDRs) are widely deployed in high-stakes domains, yet often perpetuate discrimination against minority subgroups due to biased training data. To address this, we propose a novel framework that enforces both demographic parity (DP) and—more stringently—conditional demographic parity (CDP) during optimal IDR estimation. Crucially, we prove that the constrained-optimal IDR admits an explicit, computationally efficient form: it is obtained by applying analytically tractable threshold perturbations to the unconstrained optimal IDR, depending only on subgroup-specific conditional outcome distributions. We establish non-asymptotic convergence rates: the estimated policy value converges at $O_p(n^{-1/2})$, and the fairness violation (DP/CDP gap) also attains $O_p(n^{-1/2})$. Extensive simulations and an application to the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment demonstrate that our method achieves near-optimal utility while reducing CDP violation by up to 68% over baselines and eliminating racial disparity in insurance enrollment decisions.

item.title: Balancing Privacy-Quality-Efficiency in Federated Learning through Round-Based Interleaving of Protection Techniques
item.description: Federated learning (FL) faces a persistent tripartite trade-off among privacy, model quality, and system efficiency. To address this, we propose **Alt-FL**, a novel FL framework that dynamically interleaves Differential Privacy (DP), Homomorphic Encryption (HE), and synthetic data generation *across training rounds*. Alt-FL introduces three adaptive strategies: Privacy Interleaving (PI), Synthetic Interleaving with DP (SI/DP), and Synthetic Interleaving with HE (SI/HE). We evaluate Alt-FL against four state-of-the-art gradient reconstruction attacks (DLG, IG, WCAH, Robbing the Fed) on CIFAR-10 and Fashion-MNIST using LeNet-5. Crucially, we adopt an *attacker-centric evaluation framework* that fairly compares empirical attack success rates across DP- and HE-based defenses. Results show PI achieves the best privacy-quality-efficiency balance under high privacy requirements (e.g., ε ≤ 2.0), reducing attack success rate by 63% with only 1.7% accuracy drop; SI/DP excels at moderate privacy (ε ≈ 4–8), offering 37% less communication and 2.1× faster training. Our work provides a configurable, empirically grounded foundation for selecting privacy-preserving FL methods under diverse constraints.

item.title: Deep Learning-Driven Friendly Jamming for Secure Multicarrier ISAC Under Channel Uncertainty
item.description: This paper proposes a deep learning-driven friendly jamming framework for secure multicarrier ISAC under channel uncertainty and unknown eavesdropper location. Unlike conventional FJ methods requiring Eve’s CSI or precise AoA, our approach leverages **radar echo feedback** to guide directional jamming without explicit eavesdropper information. We design a **radar-aware neural network** that jointly optimizes beamforming and jamming by integrating a novel **nonparametric f-divergence-based Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) estimator**, ensuring Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) compliance even with noisy AoA estimates. For efficiency, we adopt a **quantized tensor train encoder**, reducing model size by >100× with negligible performance loss (<0.3% secrecy rate drop). A **non-overlapping secure sub-band scheme** further isolates communication resources. Simulations show the method achieves **3.8× higher average secrecy rate**, **BLER < 10⁻⁴**, and strong robustness against CSI mismatch and angular errors—demonstrating a practical, learning-enabled physical-layer security solution for real-world ISAC systems.

item.title: Semantic Communication-Enhanced Split Federated Learning for Vehicular Networks: Architecture, Challenges, and Case Study
item.description: This paper proposes **Semantic Communication-enhanced U-Shaped Split Federated Learning (SC-USFL)** to address communication bottlenecks and label privacy risks in vehicular edge intelligence. By integrating semantic communication into SFL, SC-USFL employs a pre-trained, parameter-frozen Semantic Communication Module (SCM) to compress and transmit only task-relevant semantic features—rather than high-dimensional intermediate representations—over the uplink from vehicles to the edge server. A Network Status Monitor (NSM) enables real-time adaptive compression rate adjustment under dynamic wireless conditions. Crucially, the U-shaped architecture localizes all label-sensitive computations (e.g., classification heads) on vehicles, eliminating label leakage by design. Evaluation shows SC-USFL reduces uplink traffic by 42.7% with <1.3% accuracy degradation versus baseline SFL, while inherently preserving label privacy. The work establishes a principled synergy between semantic communication and split federated learning for resource-constrained vehicular networks.

item.title: Differential Privacy in Two-Layer Networks: How DP-SGD Harms Fairness and Robustness
item.description: Differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) is widely adopted for training models on sensitive data, yet it consistently degrades accuracy, fairness, and adversarial robustness—phenomena lacking rigorous theoretical grounding in modern non-convex networks. This paper introduces a **feature-centric analytical framework** for two-layer ReLU CNNs, establishing test loss bounds governed by the **Feature-to-Noise Ratio (FNR)**. We prove that privacy noise impairs feature learning: (1) *class- and subgroup-imbalanced FNRs cause disparate impact*; (2) *semantic long-tail samples suffer disproportionately higher error—even within the same class—due to lower intrinsic FNR*; and (3) *noise injection degrades gradient alignment, amplifying vulnerability to adversarial attacks*. Crucially, we show that public pre-training followed by private fine-tuning **fails to guarantee improvement under feature distribution shift**, challenging its common heuristic use. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks (ImageNet-100, CelebA) validate all theoretical claims.

item.title: Federated Heterogeneous Language Model Optimization for Hybrid Automatic Speech Recognition
item.description: This paper addresses the critical challenge of optimizing heterogeneous language models (LMs)—mixing n-gram and neural variants—in federated hybrid automatic speech recognition (ASR). We formalize *Federated Heterogeneous LM Optimization* and propose a novel **match-and-merge paradigm**, featuring two algorithms: (1) the **Genetic Match-and-Merge Algorithm (GMMA)**, which evolves optimal LM pairs via genetic operations and fuses them via KL-divergence-weighted averaging; and (2) the **Reinforced Match-and-Merge Algorithm (RMMA)**, which uses PPO-based reinforcement learning to maximize CER reduction during matching and fusion. Evaluated on seven OpenSLR datasets, RMMA achieves the lowest average Character Error Rate (4.21%), outperforms baselines in generalization (+12.7% cross-domain accuracy), and converges up to **7× faster than GMMA**, demonstrating strong scalability and privacy preservation for real-world ASR systems.

item.title: Can LLMs Capture Expert Uncertainty? A Comparative Analysis of Value Alignment in Ethnographic Qualitative Research
item.description: This study evaluates whether large language models (LLMs) can capture *expert-level uncertainty* when identifying human values in ethnographic interviews—using the Schwartz Theory of Basic Values as a structured framework. We compare outputs from 7 LLMs (including Qwen, GPT-4, and Llama-3) against expert annotations across set-based (F1, Jaccard), rank-based (RBO), and distributional (KL divergence, uncertainty entropy) metrics. Results show LLMs approach human performance on set matching (F1: 0.72–0.81) but underperform substantially on value ranking (mean RBO: 0.49), revealing a critical gap in modeling *relative importance*. Crucially, LLMs exhibit systematic uncertainty biases—overemphasizing Security and Conformity while underrepresenting Self-Direction and Stimulation—diverging markedly from experts’ context-sensitive uncertainty patterns. Among single models, Qwen-2-72B achieves strongest alignment with expert value distributions (lowest KL = 0.18) and highest uncertainty similarity. Ensemble methods consistently improve performance: Borda Count boosts RBO by 12.3%; Majority Vote raises F1 to 0.84. These findings underscore that LLMs are not value-neutral tools—they encode measurable value priors—and highlight the necessity of *uncertainty-aware evaluation* for trustworthy AI collaboration in qualitative social science.

item.title: FireBench: Evaluating Instruction Following in Enterprise and API-Driven LLM Applications
item.description: FireBench is the first instruction-following benchmark explicitly designed for enterprise and API-driven LLM applications, where strict adherence to output formats (e.g., JSON schema), content constraints (e.g., field omission rules), and procedural logic (e.g., multi-step API orchestration) is essential for production reliability. Unlike existing benchmarks focused on chat-style natural language generation, FireBench is grounded in real-world usage patterns across information extraction, customer support automation, and coding agents—covering 6 core capability dimensions and 2,437 high-quality, human-annotated samples. We evaluate 11 state-of-the-art LLMs and identify critical gaps: while proprietary models excel at format fidelity, over 60% of their failures stem from ignoring implicit constraints (e.g., “output JSON only, no prose”); open-weight models show stronger step ordering but weaker API protocol understanding; and fine-tuning improves domain-specific accuracy at the cost of cross-task robustness. FireBench is fully open-sourced at fire-bench.com with evaluation tooling, failure diagnostics, and contribution pathways to support model selection, developer debugging, and community-driven advancement of enterprise LLM evaluation.

item.title: From Unfamiliar to Familiar: Detecting Pre-training Data via Gradient Deviations in Large Language Models
item.description: Pre-training data detection is critical for copyright compliance and benchmark contamination mitigation in LLMs. Existing likelihood- or fine-tuning–based methods suffer from corpus frequency bias or strong dependence on fine-tuning data similarity. We observe that during training, samples evolve from *unfamiliar* to *familiar*, exhibiting systematic gradient deviations: smaller update magnitudes, distinct parameter update locations (especially across FFN vs. Attention modules), and sharper neuron activation. Leveraging this, we propose **Gradient Deviation Score (GDS)**—a training-free, pre-training–only membership inference method. GDS constructs gradient profiles capturing magnitude, module-wise location, and activation concentration of parameter updates, then feeds them into a lightweight classifier. Evaluated on five public datasets, GDS achieves state-of-the-art AUC (up to +15.6 pts over baselines) and significantly improved cross-dataset transferability (+32.4% average F1 gain). Interpretability analysis confirms stable, separable gradient feature distributions between members and non-members, enabling scalable, practical detection.

item.title: Beyond the Context Window: A Cost-Performance Analysis of Fact-Based Memory vs. Long-Context LLMs for Persistent Agents
item.description: This paper presents the first cost-performance analysis comparing fact-based memory systems against long-context LLMs for persistent conversational agents. We benchmark a Mem0-based fact memory system and long-context GPT-5-mini across three memory-centric tasks—LongMemEval, LoCoMo, and PersonaMemv2—measuring both factual recall accuracy and cumulative API cost under realistic prompt caching. Results show that while long-context LLMs achieve higher recall on LongMemEval (+18.7%) and LoCoMo (+12.3%), the fact memory system matches performance on PersonaMemv2 (within −2.1%), where stable, flat-typed attributes dominate. Crucially, our cost model reveals divergent scaling behaviors: long-context inference incurs per-turn costs growing linearly with context length—even with caching—whereas fact memory’s read cost stabilizes after an initial write phase. At 100k tokens, fact memory becomes cheaper after ~10 turns; the breakeven point decreases further with longer contexts. These findings establish a concrete, operationally actionable trade-off criterion for production deployment.

item.title: Advanced cryptography from lattice isomorphism—new constructions of IBE and FHE
item.description: We initiate the systematic adaptation of advanced lattice-based cryptographic techniques—previously developed for LWE—to the emerging lattice-isomorphism-based cryptography (introduced by Ducas and van Woerden, Eurocrypt 2022). Specifically, we construct the first identity-based encryption (IBE) and (leveled) fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) schemes whose security rests solely on the hardness of a natural variant of the Lattice Isomorphism Problem (LIP). Our constructions are modular and generic: they leverage “nice” lattices with sufficient symmetry and adapt foundational frameworks—Gentry–Peikert–Vaikuntanathan’s IBE blueprint and Gentry–Sahai–Waters’ FHE blueprint—to the LIP setting without requiring noise distributions or algebraic rings. Security is proven in the standard model under a clean, worst-case LIP assumption. This work establishes LIP as a viable foundation for advanced cryptographic primitives beyond basic encryption.

item.title: Model Extraction of Convolutional Neural Networks with Max-Pooling
item.description: Model extraction attacks aim to recover neural network parameters via black-box queries. While effective for fully connected ReLU networks, extraction of structured CNNs—especially those with max-pooling—remains underexplored due to weight sharing, locality, and the information-limited nature of max-pooling. This work studies soft-label extraction of ReLU CNNs with max-pooling. We show that max-pooling critical points encode *differences* between convolutional neuron activations; leveraging the shift-invariance of convolution, we reconstruct kernels from these differences. Using receptive-field analysis, we design efficient noise-resilient methods to localize critical points—avoiding costly reduction to sparse fully connected models. Experiments on a VGG-style CNN trained on CIFAR-10 confirm accurate weight recovery (<3.2% kernel error) and precise critical-point localization in convolutional layers.

item.title: Icefish: Practical zk-SNARKs for Verifiable Genomics
item.description: This paper introduces **Icefish**, the first practical zk-SNARK framework for verifiable genomics. We address a critical gap: while MPC and FHE enable private genomic computation, they lack *publicly verifiable correctness*—essential for scientific reproducibility and clinical trust. Icefish provides efficient zero-knowledge proofs for core genomic operations, notably accelerating sequence alignment proofs by **30×** over prior art. We design storage-efficient Merkle indexes that asymptotically reduce proof size for large-scale genomic data. We demonstrate two end-to-end applications: (**1**) Verifiable Genome-Wide Association Studies (vGWAS), enabling third-party verification of statistical results over authenticated datasets in **under 20 minutes**, matching real-world study scales; and (**2**) Verifiable CRISPR Eligibility—the first zk-SNARK application in gene editing—allowing users to prove their genome satisfies on-target/off-target safety criteria **without revealing any DNA sequence**. Icefish bridges cryptographic theory and genomic practice, enabling trustworthy, privacy-preserving biomedical computation.

item.title: Semigroup Action Problems and Their Uses in Post-Quantum Cryptography
item.description: This survey presents the Semigroup Action Problem (SAP) as a pivotal generalization of the Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP), bridging foundational algebraic cryptography and modern NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) standardization. SAP replaces group actions with semigroup actions—where invertibility and identity are not required—yielding hardness assumptions inherently resistant to Shor’s algorithm. We formalize SAP’s mathematical framework, contrast it with classical group-based assumptions, and construct secure generalizations of Diffie-Hellman and ElGamal protocols whose security reduces directly to SAP. Crucially, we demonstrate SAP’s renaissance in next-generation digital signatures: several NIST PQC finalists and candidates—including SQIsign, CSIDH-based schemes, and novel matrix semigroup proposals—rely on SAP or its concrete instantiations (e.g., ideal class group actions, isogeny path problems). Our unified algebraic analysis highlights SAP as a unifying paradigm for efficient, compact, quantum-resistant signatures.

item.title: Compact HQC with new (un)balance
item.description: Hamming Quasi-Cyclic (HQC), a NIST-standardized code-based KEM, traditionally balances bandwidth, efficiency, and security under two restrictive assumptions: (1) decryption failure rate (DFR) is set heuristically to $<2^{-\lambda}$, ignoring attack-aware bounds; and (2) the error polynomials $(\mathbf{r}_1,\mathbf{r}_2,\mathbf{e})$ are forced to be *balanced* in Hamming weight. This paper introduces **Unbalanced HQC (UHQC)**, a conservative redesign that lifts both restrictions. We first formalize the strongest DFR attack and derive an *attack-aware upper bound* on secure DFR under realistic query/time limits. Second, we quantify how *unbalanced weight distributions* of $(\mathbf{r}_1,\mathbf{r}_2,\mathbf{e})$ jointly reduce DFR and preserve concrete ISD security—enabling tighter parameter choices. Optimized UHQC parameters achieve **10–12% bandwidth reduction and 6–8% runtime improvement** across all NIST security levels, while maintaining rigorous resistance against state-of-the-art ISD and DFR attacks.

item.title: A Resource-Efficient Hardware Accelerator for Large-Size NTT via Algorithm–Architecture Co-Design
item.description: Large-size Number Theoretic Transforms (NTTs) are critical for zero-knowledge proofs (ZKPs), yet hardware acceleration faces bottlenecks in twiddle factor delivery, coefficient transpose overhead, and memory–compute coupling. We propose **RENTT**, a resource-efficient FPGA accelerator co-designed with decomposition-aware data movement and hierarchical memory. Key innovations include: (1) a precomputation-based twiddle management scheme eliminating on-the-fly generation and reducing on-chip storage; (2) a burst-optimized fused transpose that embeds reordering into twiddle multiplication, cutting off-chip accesses; and (3) a decoupled, multi-banked on-chip memory supporting configurable PE counts (16–64) and NTT sizes ($2^{20}$ to $2^{28}$). Implemented on Xilinx Alveo U280, RENTT computes a $2^{28}$-point NTT over 256-bit fields in **1.52 seconds** using 16 PEs—achieving **2.64× speedup**, **46.4% fewer DSPs**, and **3.58× lower area-time product** versus the state-of-the-art SAM baseline.

item.title: Naor-Yung Transform for IND-CCA Probing Security with Lattice Instantiations
item.description: This work introduces the first probing-adversarial security framework for public-key encryption, where an adversary can adaptively probe bounded intermediate values in secret-key algorithms (KeyGen and Dec). We formalize two new security notions—**IND-CPA-Probe** and **IND-CCA-Probe**—and prove a *probing-secure generalization of the Naor-Yung transform*: given any IND-CPA-Probe secure base scheme and a probing-secure PRF/MAC, the transformed scheme achieves IND-CCA-Probe security. Concretely, we instantiate this framework lattice-based: **Rutile**, a masking-friendly IND-CPA-Probe scheme inspired by Kyber but replacing its central binomial sampling with sum-of-uniforms (SoU); and **Topaz**, its IND-CCA-Probe secure variant via the NY transform—eliminating Kyber’s FO transform and its masking inefficiency. Rutile and Topaz retain Kyber-level efficiency while enabling practical high-order masking, bridging provable probing security and post-quantum deployment.

item.title: The Art of Linearization: From a KZG’s Trick to a General Commitment Framework
item.description: We formalize the widely used linearization technique from Marlin and Plonk into a general cryptographic primitive: the **Linearization Polynomial Commitment Scheme (LPCS)**. We instantiate it as **LinKZG**, proving its weak extractability under a target-group variant of the Adaptive Root Strong Diffie-Hellman (ARSDH) assumption in the Random Oracle Model (ROM), and show that Plonk remains secure under the same assumption. Crucially, we provide a generic construction: *any homomorphic polynomial commitment scheme can be transformed into an LPCS*—enabling linearization beyond KZG (e.g., for Bulletproofs-PCS or Dory). This unlocks efficiency gains for numerous SNARKs by reducing opening communication and verification cost, all without new trust assumptions or setup overhead.

item.title: Adaptively Secure, Universally Composable Distributed Generation of Discrete-Logarithm Based Keys
item.description: We present the first adaptively secure, universally composable (UC) distributed key generation (DKG) protocols for discrete-logarithm-based keys—without erasure, inconsistent players, interactive assumptions, or oracle-aided simulation. All protocols are proven secure in the random oracle model under the DDH assumption and rely on synchrony for output guarantees. Our main contributions are: (1) a 3-round UC adaptive-secure DKG with guaranteed output delivery under honest majority; (2) a 2-round UC adaptive-secure *committed* DKG realizing a novel committed-DKG functionality, tolerating full corruption ($t = n-1$) with identifiable abort; (3) a 3-round committed DKG achieving guaranteed output under honest majority; and (4) an extremely simple threshold Schnorr protocol in the committed-DKG-hybrid model—yielding, respectively, a 3-round UC adaptive-secure threshold Schnorr with identifiable abort (full corruption) and a 4-round one with guaranteed output (honest majority). These results significantly strengthen the security and efficiency of practical threshold cryptography.

item.title: Libra: Pattern-Scheduling Co-Optimization for Cross-Scheme FHE Code Generation over GPGPU
item.description: Libra is a compiler framework that enables efficient cross-scheme fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) code generation for GPGPUs via pattern-scheduling co-optimization. To overcome the challenge of mapping heterogeneous FHE operators (e.g., BFV, CKKS, BGV) onto massively parallel GPUs, Libra introduces a unified cross-scheme IR abstracting common computational patterns and dynamically optimizes scheme selection and fusion based on holistic execution costs—including switching overheads and primitive-level GPU performance models. It further bridges high-level computation semantics (e.g., batching, data reuse) with low-level GPU execution plans (e.g., warp scheduling, shared-memory tiling, coalesced memory access). Evaluated on NVIDIA A100, Libra achieves up to **270× speedup on microbenchmarks** and **19× on real-world applications**, while improving GPU compute utilization by **44%** and memory bandwidth utilization by **36.1%**, outperforming state-of-the-art cross-scheme FHE systems.

item.title: Noise-aware Client Selection for carbon-efficient Federated Learning via Gradient Norm Thresholding
item.description: Training large-scale neural networks is energy-intensive, and carbon-aware Federated Learning (FL) leverages geographically distributed renewable energy to reduce AI’s environmental footprint. However, existing client selection strategies—designed to align training with carbon availability—often inadvertently select clients with noisy or low-quality data due to FL’s privacy constraints, degrading model accuracy and convergence. To address this, we propose **Noise-aware Client Selection via Gradient Norm Thresholding (NCCS)**, a lightweight, plug-and-play module that enhances robustness without accessing raw data. NCCS introduces probing rounds to compute local gradient L2 norms; clients whose gradients exceed a dynamic threshold—indicative of label noise or poor data quality—are filtered out before aggregation. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10-C and FEMNIST-Noise show that conventional loss-based selection harms accuracy by up to 8.2%, while NCCS improves final accuracy by 5.6–9.3%, accelerates convergence by 22%, and maintains 21–34% carbon reduction versus baselines. Our work bridges carbon efficiency and statistical robustness in practical FL deployments.

item.title: CAM-LDS: Cyber Attack Manifestations for Automatic Interpretation of System Logs and Security Alerts
item.description: Log analysis remains a bottleneck in security operations due to volume, heterogeneity, and unstructured text—yet existing automated methods rely heavily on expert-crafted rules, parsers, or features, lacking semantic understanding and explainability. To enable LLM-driven log interpretation, we introduce **CAM-LDS**, the first open-source, reproducible log dataset explicitly designed for attack manifestation analysis. CAM-LDS covers **81 ATT&CK techniques across 13 tactics**, derived from **7 end-to-end attack scenarios** executed in a fully open testbed and collected from **18 diverse log sources** (e.g., Sysmon, Suricata, Wazuh). Crucially, all logs are curated to reflect *direct attack causality*, annotated with technique IDs and contextual metadata (command observability, event frequency, IDS alerts). In an illustrative LLM case study (Llama 3-8B), the model correctly identified the ground-truth ATT&CK technique for **~33% of attack steps**, and provided semantically adequate predictions for another **~33%**, demonstrating both the promise of LLM-based log interpretation and the utility of CAM-LDS as a foundational benchmark.

item.title: From Threat Intelligence to Firewall Rules: Semantic Relations in Hybrid AI Agent and Expert System Architectures
item.description: Web security requires rapid, trustworthy responses to evolving cyber threats, yet purely neural AI approaches lack explainability and reliability for sensitive operational tasks like firewall configuration. This paper introduces a hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture that leverages **hypernym-hyponym semantic relations** extracted from Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports to drive automated, auditable security control generation. Our multi-agent system parses CTI text, infers hierarchical threat semantics (e.g., “Emotet” → “banking trojan” → “malware”), and synthesizes executable CLIPS rules for an expert system that generates validated firewall rules (e.g., iptables/ACL). Experiments on 1,247 real-world CTI reports show our semantic retrieval strategy achieves **92.3% F1**, outperforming BM25, Sentence-BERT, and zero-shot LLM baselines; the generated rules achieve **98.6% threat blocking rate with only 0.4% false positives**, demonstrating superior precision and operational trustworthiness. This work bridges semantic intelligence and executable security policy—enabling explainable, expert-augmented automation in SOAR pipelines.

item.title: Structure-Aware Distributed Backdoor Attacks in Federated Learning
item.description: Federated learning (FL) preserves data privacy but introduces stealthy backdoor vulnerabilities during distributed model updates. Prior work largely assumes uniform perturbation effectiveness across architectures—a flawed premise ignoring structural influence. This paper pioneers a *structure-aware* analysis of the coupling between model architecture and backdoor perturbations. We propose two novel metrics: the **Structural Responsiveness Score (SRS)**, quantifying architectural sensitivity to perturbations, and the **Structural Compatibility Coefficient (SCC)**, measuring preference for fractal perturbations. Leveraging them, we design the **Topology-aware Fractal Injection (TFI)** framework. Experiments across 6 model families show that multi-path architectures (e.g., ResNet) amplify and retain fractal perturbations even at 1% poisoning ratio, while low-SCC models severely constrain attack success. Crucially, SCC strongly predicts attack success (*r* = 0.93), enabling proactive perturbation survivability estimation. Our findings establish that backdoor efficacy in FL is governed not only by perturbation design or poisoning intensity, but by the *triadic interaction among model structure, perturbation topology, and aggregation mechanism*—providing foundational insights for structure-aware defense.

item.title: T2S-Bench & Structure-of-Thought: Benchmarking and Prompting Comprehensive Text-to-Structure Reasoning
item.description: This paper introduces **Structure-of-Thought (SoT)**, a prompting paradigm that explicitly guides LLMs to generate structured intermediate representations (e.g., JSON schemas, dependency graphs, reasoning trees) before final output—mimicking human structural reasoning. Built on this insight, we propose **T2S-Bench**, the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating text-to-structure capabilities, comprising 1.8K expert-validated samples across 6 scientific domains and 32 structural types. Evaluations on 45 mainstream models reveal critical gaps: average multi-hop reasoning accuracy is only 52.1%, and even the strongest model achieves just 58.1% node-level extraction accuracy end-to-end. SoT alone delivers +5.7% average gain across eight diverse tasks on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct; fine-tuning on T2S-Bench lifts this to +8.6%. These results underscore the necessity of explicit structure induction and establish SoT and T2S-Bench as complementary, scalable levers for advancing structured language understanding. Dataset and code are publicly available at https://t2s-bench.github.io/T2S-Bench-Page/.

item.title: Interactive Proofs for Batch Polynomial Evaluation
item.description: We present concretely efficient interactive proofs for batch polynomial evaluation. For evaluating a degree-$d$ univariate polynomial at $m$ points, our $\mathsf{MA}$ protocol achieves *linear-time verification*: the prover sends only $d-1$ field elements, and the verifier performs $O(m+d)$ field operations—eliminating the $\log^2(m+d)$ factor of prior art. We extend this to the general multi-polynomial, multi-point setting via an $\mathsf{AMA}$ protocol with the same verifier complexity. Our protocols improve verification time in fundamental primitives—including sumcheck over large domains and polynomial quotienting—reducing STIR’s (CRYPTO 2024) verifier runtime to match that of WHIR (EUROCRYPT 2025). As a direct application, we show any univariate polynomial commitment scheme can be transformed, in a black-box manner, into one supporting batch openings at $m$ points with only constant overhead over a single opening.

item.title: Short Signatures from DDH without Pairings or Random Oracles
item.description: We present two short signature schemes based solely on the polynomial-hard Decisional Diffie-Hellman (DDH) assumption. Both operate in standard prime-order groups—**no pairings are required**, and security is proven **in the standard model**, without random oracles. The first scheme achieves selective unforgeability, while the second attains full existential unforgeability under adaptive chosen-message attacks. Signatures consist of only a single group element, and public keys are constant-size. Security reductions are tight and direct: forging a signature implies efficiently solving DDH with non-negligible advantage. This work eliminates long-standing dependencies on bilinear maps or heuristic random oracles, offering a simpler, more efficient, and foundationally robust alternative for lightweight authentication.

item.title: Trace: Complete Client-Side Account Access Logging
item.description: Despite advances in authentication, account compromise remains pervasive, and users lack trustworthy ways to audit device access—especially under privacy regulations prohibiting static device identifiers. Prior client-side encrypted logging (CSAL) schemes fail to guarantee *complete* log retrieval, risking missed adversarial accesses. We present **Trace**, a new CSAL system that achieves *full* logging while preserving privacy, backward compatibility, and verifiable device attribution. Trace generates cryptographically signed, encrypted log entries *client-side* for each authentication, stores them with an independent logging service, and ensures only the user can decrypt and inspect them—leaving the web service entirely unaware of logging. Unlike prior work, Trace is formally analyzed against malicious adversaries and supports scalable deployment: our prototype processes **over 10,000 authentications per second on a single core**. Trace reconciles accountability and privacy without modifying existing authentication infrastructure.
